title: Pain Coach
overview: A pain and lifestyle tracking platform for post-operative patients, paired with a companion dashboard for clinicians. Patients log their pain levels, activity, and lifestyle factors to better understand and reduce their long-term pain.
coverImageAlt: Pain Coach app dashboard showing pain tracking metrics

design.description1: Inspired by the lack of accessible digital tooling in chronic pain management, I set out to build a clinical platform that could support real patients and clinicians in a highly regulated, trust-sensitive environment — one where reliability and data integrity are non-negotiable.
design.description2: Built as a TypeScript/Vue monorepo using Nuxt and Nitro, Pain Coach combines a responsive patient-facing interface with a robust backend of over 600 API endpoints. The architecture supports multiple independent clinics, each with their own programs, and role-based access control across clinicians, admins, and patients.

development.description1: PostgreSQL underpins the platform's data layer, with an organisation model that isolates each clinic's data from the others. All API boundaries are validated with Zod schemas, and strict role- and permission-based access control ensures clinicians, admins, and patients only ever access data within their own organisation.
development.description2: The backend runs a pipeline of around 20 scheduled cron jobs handling async image processing, multi-channel clinical form delivery (email, SMS, and push notifications), and subscription billing lifecycle management via Stripe and Xero. Third-party integrations span AWS SES, S3, and SNS, Google Maps, and Cloudflare R2 — with a clinical trial currently running at the University of Technology Sydney.

title: Penny Projects
overview: A non-profit raising funds to make a lasting impact on Riverside Secondary High School in Zambia and its surrounding community.
coverImageAlt: People gathering water at a well, with the Penny Projects logo
demoVideoAlt: Short demonstration of the Penny Projects website landing section

design.description1: Designed in close collaboration with a graphic designer, working from my direction on the site's structure and layout. The result is a simple, easy-to-navigate experience with a strong brand identity that keeps the focus on the organisation's content and the stories of the people it helps.

development.description1: Built with Nuxt 3 as a hybrid of SSR and SPA — rendered on the server, then hydrated on the client — so the site is SEO-friendly and fast to load while keeping the smoothness of a single-page app. Considerable care went into faithfully realising the designer's vision and ensuring a fully responsive experience across every device.

title: Galactic Asteroid Miner
overview: A 2D arcade-style space mining game built in Unity. You pilot a mining ship and collect as many asteroids as you can while managing your fuel and health. It was my first released title, after more than six years of tinkering in Unity.
coverImageAlt: Space station orbiting a star on the game's title screen

design.description1: I had a clear visual direction before the gameplay even existed. With programming as my main focus, I kept the art deliberately simple and built around solid colours — giving the game a distinct aesthetic that players described as 'smooth gameplay, and a nostalgic experience.'

development.description1: Built in Unity with C# and .NET, using Unity's UI system for the menus and its physics engine tuned to model Newtonian gravity, with code following SOLID and DRY principles throughout.
development.description2: The standout technical features include infinite procedural generation without a noise function like Perlin, an occlusion culling system that only renders objects within a set view radius, and spatial hashing that splits the world into manageable chunks — all underpinned by a clean architecture built for extensibility. I'm currently rewriting the networking from the ground up to add multiplayer with Mirror.

Red Dwarf.imageAlt: Galactic Asteroid Miner red dwarf system

White Dwarf.imageAlt: Galactic Asteroid Miner white dwarf system

title: Upbranched
overview: A lightweight social platform built for independent communities. Users create and join 'branches' — collections of user-moderated groups — and share text, image, and video posts, vote them up or down, and reply through nested comments. Currently in development, with a release planned soon.
coverImageAlt: User posts on the Upbranched platform homepage
demoVideoAlt: A short video demonstration of the Upbranched platform

design.description1: Using Reddit as a starting point, I focused on the areas I felt it was lacking — building something more visually polished, easier to navigate, and far more customisable for the people running their own communities.
design.description2: Nuxt 3 powers a fast, maintainable frontend that blends SSG and SPA rendering to stay responsive and highly SEO-friendly. The stack pairs Vite with the latest Vue 3 and TypeScript features, with Sass for structured, nested styling.

development.description1: A social platform needs structured, relational data, so I chose PostgreSQL over the non-relational databases I'd worked with before. Every query is written in plain SQL, and all user-submitted content is validated and sanitised to guard against SQL injection.
development.description2: To keep communities healthy, I integrated Google's Perspective API for automated comment moderation. Other features include full Auth0 authentication with Google and GitHub, a custom image uploader and compressor, and an Imgur web-scraper for cross-posting content while easing the load on Upbranched's own storage.

title: Ochre & Ivy
overview: A fully custom blogging platform built for a school teacher who wanted an online home to share their passion for all things natural and organic. Having found Squarespace and Wix too costly and restrictive, I built the site from scratch in TypeScript — no framework — with a built-in CMS and a user-friendly admin panel.
coverImageAlt: Ochre & Ivy blog homepage with hand-drawn illustration and typography

design.description1: The brief called for something simple and minimal. With little copy across the site, it was essential that the blog and recipe content stayed front and centre and easy to digest — so I drew on my graphic design background to craft a brand identity that felt both unique and memorable.
design.description2: Wherever others might reach for JavaScript or a library to build carousels and animations, I opted for CSS instead. That kept the site lightweight and fast, and made for a more accessible experience for users with disabilities.

development.description1: As a deliberate learning exercise, I built the site from the ground up without a framework — a deep dive into how websites work under the hood and how to tune them for performance. The result scores 100% on Google Lighthouse across both mobile and desktop.
development.description2: At its core is a built-in CMS that lets the client create, update, and delete content without ever leaving the site, secured behind user authentication and powered by EditorJS. Content is saved as JSON, validated on both the client and server to prevent code injection, then parsed and rendered — giving the client a flexible, familiar editing experience with no need to touch HTML or markdown.

title: The Lipstick Hippy
overview: An online store for natural and organic health food products, built on Wix. Now retired, the project was an end-to-end exercise in delivering a polished, self-manageable storefront and a complete brand identity for a small business.
coverImageAlt: The Lipstick Hippy online store homepage with an organic products display

design.description1: Starting from a template, I customised the store to fit the client's needs and gave it a unified brand identity within the first few weeks. Across seven months I handled all of the brand work myself — logo design, colour palette, graphics, and email marketing campaigns — and, where the platform couldn't support a custom recipe builder, designed a bespoke Photoshop template the client could use to send recipes to customers.

development.description1: I built the store in Wix's low-code editor so the client could manage day-to-day changes themselves without incurring extra cost. I rapidly prototyped the design and functionality, then worked closely with the client to teach them the platform and handle larger updates.
development.description2: The site brought together a range of plugins and ongoing maintenance — email marketing campaigns, a blog, a full store with custom shipping and payment options, an authentication and login system, and a subscription-only member portal.
